And somehow, you know, my thought process was always, like, our marketing is so bad.

But I set out to do that.
And so I somehow convinced them to let me sign up the first site ever that was outside of IBM.com, securityintelligence.com.
And it grew to be first year did like 17 million in revenue from like just a few hundred thousand dollars in investments.

And it just worked.
And they're sunsetting this year, but it survived like 15 years successfully with that even after I left.

Like, and, and they're very similar.
It very much like.
If you were starting a media company today, except it's funded by this company, right?

Yeah.

was three months before my, was going to be my paternity leave.

God.

so so so you.

we have now 50 people in the company and over 50 customers, almost 50 customers.

But, but I think like, the, the main thing is, as a CMO, I always had more variable budget, if you will, than any other department, I always was really frustrated with most service providers, I always had agencies and freelancers working for me.
And I was always like, frustrated in finding them, right, especially around content, and but almost
Every part of marketing relies a lot on service providers, and we're always buying more tools, but then the team would never use it, and so it's always kind of like the same thing, right?
And so for me, I always wanted to build something that kind of solved it more from an outcomes, it's like the things I wish I had, right?
And what I wish I had was like, I don't want the tool, and I don't want a half-baked solution.

I don't want to have to hire a head of content, then writers and editors, then an SEO consultant, then an agency for this, and, you know, a graphic designer for that, and then stitch it all together and be the glue that holds it all together, you know?
And so that's why, like, we kind of focus on outcomes first, and I think content just happens to be, like, just a massive, massive space, right?
Like, and so, like, our, like, I don't know if you saw the post that I did, but, like, we're, like, less than 30-day sales cycle, about 160,000 ACV, and, like...
We have a 48% win rate.

And it's mostly because I think of just like, we hit the mark on like how to package it and how to deliver and how to make it like, not too cheap, but like cheap enough or inexpensive enough for the outcome and the quality.
That is such a no brainer for people, you know, and that's why we're booked out through May.
But the idea is like what we start with is editorial and content and programmatic.

And so the idea is mostly like you want to create content at scale in a really high quality way to essentially increase like your surface area, your like almost like your sellable surface area, right?

And when you do that now, once you have this content engine working, then you can start building distribution, whether it's through social, newsletters, you know, lead magnets, like ways to convert people or drive directly to signups, right?
And then taking all those signals and subscriptions and converting them to sales, right?
And, and, and but.
And then the way we're doing that is mostly through, like, experts.

know, like, Dave billed Fanatics to $600 million a year.

Like, and so that kind of, like, fast forwards a little bit.

Generated by our experts, right?
Like created by our experts.

And, like, and I think the form factor for knowledge work hasn't, like, been done right.

And so, like...

And then the task hub, right, is kind of the orchestration layer between understanding the people and the experts and then understanding, like, where in the steps should it be, you know, reviewed by an expert or a human in the loop.
And then the content OS is like almost kind of like the presentation layer for all of this work, you know, that's fairly, fairly, fairly custom.
But, like, a good example here is like I was just on a demo call.
So it's like this one is like our deep research workflow.
And it's like, you know, this topic is BPO and e-commerce, and it will generate research questions, and then it will, like, perform deep research, and then, like, combine all of those results, right?
And this is all represented in code, and, you know, and so it's, like, it goes through, like, this entire workflow.

So, and, but then, like, we have so many different ones that we do, like, whether it's, like, a call or, you know, and so it's, like, we're just building this kind of library, and then, like, we're just running it at a massive scale.

And so, like, even for our own internal jobs to be done, so, for instance, every time somebody applies for a job, we actually parse the resume, get the job requirement, analyze it, and look at the answers to their questions, and then decide whether or not to move them ahead, you know?

come to you.

And all the way to like, you know, massive public companies.
Super, super interesting.

And then

The way you kind of deploy today is you'll have kind of a team that you assign internally to work with the customer, get them familiar with the platform, and then, you know, get kind of the workflows going and then expose the customer to your platform.
Yeah, like so today, today, it's way less on exposing the platform.
And so like, it mostly like customers experience it much like a service, right?
Like, and so we have a space where we share docs and results, we're in Slack with them.
So every week and every day, we're posting updates, we're taking all the grunt work from them, whether it's like staging the content, optimizing, refreshing the content, creating graphics for it.

And like, the way we think about it is like, we want to take over as much as possible from your plate of things that you hate doing, in addition to help with the strategy and the execution, so that like, over time, like, we'll figure out how to automate or how to streamline that process, right?
Like, so it's almost like counter to like,
So agencies, where agencies want to do the minimum possible and only the high-level stuff because the other stuff is not really core to them.
For us, we want to solve an entire problem so that they feel like, oh my god, we need to hire five different people to do what you guys do.
And then what we have is managing directors.
Like I said, they're kind of like managing partners at a law firm.
They sell, they are the experts, so they're doing product development, but then they manage pods.
And the pods are made out of like the pod lead, if you will, who is like kind of like a managing editor type of person with like a lot of experience.
And a team of like three to four, if you will, like strategists, content managers, production researchers, like, you know, a few folks that might almost be like a VA.
And so in some ways, like if you think about like almost like a pyramid of teams, like the bottom layer is the layer that will, you know, is like, like be the scale.
The least over time, if you will.

And then the expert layer, the managing director is the one that's the most strategic where we're distilling a lot of their thought processes to learn from those patterns into our workflows, know, the activities.
And then, and you just raised a Series A?
Yeah, we raised $12 million.
But we just moved super fast.

We just, I partnered with Farhan from Adrona because he was on the board of DeepGram and we just like had so much work to do and so much demand that we kind of went from like first meeting, to do money wire in 30 days and kind of like...
It's like your sales cycle, you know?
Yeah, we were just like, we were profitable.

That's not going to have an exit or it go anywhere.
Time, you know.
And so it was mostly like a seal of approval.

You know, I think when we started, were like 4 million in AR.
By the time money was wired, we were over six, you know, and we raised it 80 million posts.
It was like pretty reasonable multiples, you know, like, and especially given like, growth and profitability and all of that.
so, so like part of that is because we didn't want like early employers to come in and be like, there's no way I can exercise this.
There's like no upside here.
Like, you know, and because we're not, we don't need to hire hundreds of people.
And so it's like this year is like the most critical one.
And then the goal now is like, we're, we're sprinting towards 20 million in ARR.
And we're trying to do three things.
One, continue to scale what we're doing and validate it all the way to 20 million in ARR.
much.

hanging out won't be a problem.

then, yeah, whenever I imagine, or I don't know, in terms of, like, fundraising, how are you thinking about it going forward?

And if you're able to do that and operationalize that, then you just have a lot of compounding growth and also mitigates a lot of the risk.
And so one of the paths that...

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Introductory meeting between Marcel Santilli (GrowthX) and Anoushka Vaswani (Lightspeed Venture Partners) to discuss GrowthX's business model, growth, and future plans.](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX has rapidly grown to $7M ARR, 50 employees, and \~50 customers in less than a year](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=510.0)
  - [Two-sided business: customer-facing content services + underlying tech platform/workflow engine](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=568.0)
  - [Recently raised $12M Series A at $80M post-money valuation, aiming for $20M ARR](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=505.0)
  - [Exploring expansion into new verticals (e.g., sales) and potential future fundraising for M\&A](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=1446.0)

## Topics

### Marcel's Background and GrowthX Origin

  - [Brazilian-born, moved to Texas at 11](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=192.0)
  - [Built organic content engines throughout career (IBM, ServiceTitan, HashiCorp, Scale AI, Deepgram)](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=218.0)
  - [Started GrowthX by teaching workshops, evolved into full-service offering](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=424.0)
  - [Partnered with CTO Daniel (ex-IFTTT) to build underlying tech platform](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=485.0)

### GrowthX Business Model

  - [Two sides: customer-facing content services + underlying tech platform](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=568.0)
  - [Focuses on delivering outcomes, not just tools or partial solutions](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=622.0)
  - [Uses expert-driven "managing director" model for service delivery](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=1274.0)
  - [Underlying platform: workflow engine, runtime layer, IDE experience, services/workflows](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=796.0)
  - [Learning engine captures insights from workflow execution and expert input](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=767.0)

### Customer Base and Offering

  - [Diverse customer base: Series A startups to public companies (e.g., Webflow, Ramp, Reddit)](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=1135.0)
  - $160K ACV, 48% win rate, \<30-day sales cycle
  - [Full-service approach: strategy, execution, and "grunt work" (e.g., content staging, optimization)](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=626.0)
  - [Team structure: Managing Directors lead pods of specialists (editors, strategists, researchers)](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=1274.0)

### Growth and Funding

  - [Crossed $7M ARR, still profitable (may start burning cash due to rapid growth)](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=524.0)
  - [Raised $12M Series A at $80M post-money valuation](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=505.0)
  - [Aiming for $20M ARR, exploring new verticals (e.g., sales) and platform exposure](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=1428.0)
  - [Considering future Series B for M\&A, geographic expansion, and talent acquisition](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=1617.0)

### Future Plans and Strategy

  - [Three-pronged approach:](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=1554.0)
    1.  Scale current offering to $20M ARR
    2.  Expand into new vertical/domain (possibly sales)
    3.  Further develop and expose the underlying platform
  - [Exploring international opportunities (e.g., Mexico, UK)](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=1584.0)
  - [Considering M\&A to acquire expertise and accelerate growth in specific niches](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=1620.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Marcel and Anoushka to meet in person after GrowthX's board meeting on April 20th](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=1676.0)
  - [Explore potential collaborations with Lightspeed portfolio companies](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=1500.0)
  - [Continue discussions on GrowthX's growth strategy and future funding plans](https://fathom.video/share/pdxcz7ZXEhtwyZExE4xsmmQkXyfxEpAF?tab=summary&timestamp=1665.0)
